mnemonics.title: Truth Table Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: AND: Like a strict boss - needs EVERYTHING to be true. OR: Like a generous friend - needs at least ONE to be happy. IMPLY: Like a promise - broken only when you say yes but don't deliver.

mnemonics.title: Inference Rules Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: MODUS PONENS: 'Affirm the Antecedent' - Say P, get Q. MODUS TOLLENS: 'Deny the Consequent' - No Q, no P. HYPOTHETICAL SYLLOGISM: Chain reactions - If P then Q, If Q then R, therefore If P then R.

mnemonics.title: Key Laws Remembering
mnemonics.content: DE MORGAN: Break the line, flip the sign. Flip ∧ to ∨, flip ∨ to ∧. COMMUTATIVE: Order doesn't matter. ASSOCIATIVE: Parentheses don't matter. DISTRIBUTIVE: Multiply out or factorize.

acronyms.usage: A formula in CNF is an AND of ORs: (A ∨ B) ∧ (C ∨ D)
A formula in DNF is an OR of ANDs: (A ∧ B) ∨ (C ∧ D)
Lists all possible truth value combinations for propositional variables

visualTips.visualization: Think of truth table rows as binary counting from 0 to 2^n - 1. First column alternates every row, second column every 2 rows, third column every 4 rows.
Use a grid: AND (intersection area in Venn diagram), OR (union area). Implication as a subset relationship: P → Q means P's truth set is contained in Q's.
Think of P → Q as a promise: 'If you do P, I'll do Q'. The promise is broken (false) only when P happens (true) but Q doesn't happen (false).

analogies.analogy: Like light switches - always either ON (true) or OFF (false), never both, never neither.
AND is like needing both keys to open a lock. OR is like having either key to open a door. NOT is like flipping a switch.
Two propositions are logically equivalent if they always 'agree' - like twins who always give the same answer regardless of the question.
Tautology is like a always-win game (always true). Contradiction is like a always-lose game (always false). Contingency is like a game where winning depends on your moves.
